title: Emergency Guide: Dog with Bloated Abdomen and Inability to Urinate
meta_desc: A bloated abdomen and inability to urinate in dogs is a life-threatening emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is critical for survival.
content_html:
TL;DR: A bloated abdomen combined with an inability to urinate is a life-threatening medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention to prevent organ failure or shock. These symptoms are often signs of critical conditions like bloat (GDV) or a urinary tract obstruction.

What causes my dog to have a bloated abdomen and an inability to urinate?

A bloated abdomen combined with an inability to urinate can indicate several life-threatening conditions. The bloating may suggest Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (GDV), commonly known as "bloat," where the stomach twists and traps gas. Simultaneously, the inability to urinate may indicate a urinary tract obstruction (urolithiasis) or extreme internal pressure on the bladder. Both conditions cause extreme pain, distress, and rapid physiological decline.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog has a distended stomach and cannot pee?

  • Urgency Level: Critical / High. Yes, this is a severe medical emergency.
  • If your dog's abdomen is distended and they are straining to urinate without success, you must transport them to an emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • These conditions can lead to bladder rupture, organ failure, or circulatory shock within a matter of hours.
  • Do not wait for a regular appointment.

How can a photo or video help the emergency triage team?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a quick photo of your dog’s abdomen from a side profile and from above to help the triage team assess the severity of the distension.
  • Record a short video of your dog attempting to urinate to help the veterinarian distinguish between a physical blockage and neurological distress.
  • Providing these visuals allows the medical team to prepare life-saving equipment in advance of your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Urethral obstruction in dogs is an emergency, characterized by frequent attempts to urinate with minimal or no urine production, potentially accompanied by pain. Complete obstruction can lead to uremia within 36-48 hours, manifesting as depression, anorexia, vomiting, diarrhea, dehydration, coma, and death within 48-72 hours. Clinical signs may include pollakiuria, stranguria, hematuria, and abdominal pain. Physical examination may reveal a distended, hard, and painful bladder; in cases of bladder rupture, the bladder may be impalpable, and abdominal distention may be noted. Hyperkalemia-induced bradycardia or cardiac arrhythmias may be present. Immediate relief of the obstruction via catheterization and fluid therapy with normal saline is critical.

Chapter: Emergency, Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1527)

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. If your dog cannot urinate and has a bloated abdomen, the situation is life-threatening. Waiting even a few hours can be fatal.

faqs.question: What are other signs of Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (GDV)?
faqs.answer: Other signs include unproductive retching (trying to vomit but nothing comes up), restlessness, pacing, excessive drooling, and pale gums.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the emergency clinic?
faqs.answer: Call the clinic to let them know you are on your way with a potential bloat or obstruction case. Keep your dog as calm and still as possible during transport.
